narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 55.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for Year 1 grades at your school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—along with a similarly low Year 3 decile, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90 below the average of 3.21, a solid LSAT score of 38 that's above the usual 36.7, high family income, and your status as a male, White, full-time student, which aligns with common demographics in the applicant pool. When the model evaluated you, the biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 1 decile rank, as it stood out as the most influential element by far. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which also nudged the prediction toward failure despite being stronger than average. Your Year 3 decile came next in influence, followed closely by your undergraduate GPA, your racial background, family income, full-time status, and gender, each contributing in descending order to the overall risk assessment. In essence, while your LSAT and family income are strengths compared to peers, your below-average school deciles and GPA carried the most weight in signaling potential bar struggles relative to typical students who pass. This ranking shows how early academic performance shaped the decision more than anything else for you specifically. Looking ahead, you could realistically boost future chances by targeting academic weaknesses—perhaps through targeted tutoring to improve grades in upcoming years or retaking the LSAT for an even higher score if possible. Building a stronger GPA through additional coursework or post-undergrad achievements might also help demonstrate growth. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by focusing on consistent study habits and bar prep resources early on. With your high family support and solid LSAT foundation, you're well-positioned to turn this around for the next application cycle. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
